section.title: The Valley of Dry Bones and One Nation
section.sectionRationale: The book's most iconic vision, dry bones brought to life by the Spirit's breath, answers Israel's despair, 'our hope has perished,' with the promise of national resurrection. The chapter then joins two sticks into one, picturing a reunited people under one King, David's Son, and an everlasting covenant of peace with God's own sanctuary set among them forever.
section.thesis: What looks like a valley of hopelessly dry bones becomes, at the LORD's word and by His Spirit's breath, a vast living army, and what looks like two divided, exiled nations become one people under one Shepherd-King forever, previewing the resurrection life and the reunited, indwelt people of God secured in Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the valley of dry bones brought to life by prophesied breath; the vision's interpretation as the whole house of Israel's opened graves and God's Spirit given; two sticks joined into one, picturing the reunited nation under one King; and the everlasting covenant of peace, with God's own sanctuary set among His people forever.

units.label: The valley of dry bones
units.note: vision, prophecy to the bones, and prophecy to the breath

verses.text: The hand of the LORD was upon me, and He brought me out by His {{ruach|Spirit}} and set me down in the middle of the valley, and it was full of bones.
He led me all around among them, and I saw a great many bones on the floor of the valley, and indeed, they were very dry.

Then He asked me, “Son of man, can these bones come to life?” 
“O Lord GOD,” I replied, “only You know.”
And He said to me, “Prophesy concerning these bones and tell them, ‘{{atsamot_yeveshot|Dry bones}}, hear the word of the LORD!
This is what the Lord GOD says to these bones: I will cause breath to enter you, and you will come to life.
I will attach tendons to you and make flesh grow upon you and cover you with skin. I will put breath within you so that you will come to life. Then you will know that I am the LORD.’”
So I prophesied as I had been commanded. And as I prophesied, there was suddenly a noise, a rattling, and the bones came together, bone to bone.
As I looked on, tendons appeared on them, flesh grew, and skin covered them; but there was no breath in them.

Then He said to me, “Prophesy to the breath; prophesy, son of man, and tell the breath that this is what the Lord GOD says: Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe into these slain, so that they may live!”
So I prophesied as He had commanded me, and the breath entered them, and they came to life and stood on their feet, a vast army.

units.label: My people, I will open your graves
units.note: the vision interpreted: national resurrection and My Spirit given

verses.text: Then He said to me, “Son of man, these bones are the whole house of Israel. Look, they are saying, ‘Our bones are dried up, and our hope has perished; we are cut off.’
Therefore prophesy and tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘O My people, I will open your graves and bring you up from them, and I will bring you back to the land of Israel.
Then you, My people, will know that I am the LORD, when I open your graves and bring you up from them.
I will put My {{ruach|Spirit}} in you and you will live, and I will settle you in your own land. Then you will know that I, the LORD, have spoken, and I will do it, declares the LORD.’”

units.label: The two sticks made one
units.note: one nation, one king, cleansed forever

verses.text: Again the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“And you, son of man, take a single stick and write on it: ‘Belonging to Judah and to the Israelites associated with him.’ Then take another stick and write on it: ‘Belonging to Joseph, the stick of Ephraim, and to all the house of Israel associated with him.’
Then join them together into one stick, so that they become one in your hand.
When your people ask you, ‘Won't you explain to us what you mean by these?’

you are to tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘I will take the stick of Joseph, which is in the hand of Ephraim, and the tribes of Israel associated with him, and I will put them together with the stick of Judah. I will make them into a single stick, and they will become one in My hand.’
When the sticks on which you write are in your hand and in full view of the people,

you are to tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘I will take the Israelites out of the nations to which they have gone, and I will gather them from all around and bring them into their own land.

I will make them {{goy_echad|one nation}} in the land, on the mountains of Israel, and one king will rule over all of them. Then they will no longer be two nations and will never again be divided into two kingdoms.

They will no longer defile themselves with their idols or detestable images, or with any of their transgressions. I will save them from all their apostasies by which they sinned, and I will cleanse them. Then they will be My people, and I will be their God.

units.label: One shepherd, an everlasting covenant, My sanctuary among them
units.note: My servant David king forever, God dwelling with His people

verses.text: My servant David will be king over them, and there will be one shepherd for all of them. They will follow My ordinances and keep and observe My statutes.

They will live in the land that I gave to My servant Jacob, where your fathers lived. They will live there forever with their children and grandchildren, and My servant David will be their prince forever.
And I will make a covenant of peace with them; it will be an everlasting covenant. I will establish them and multiply them, and I will set My sanctuary among them forever.
My dwelling place will be with them; I will be their God, and they will be My people.
Then the nations will know that I the LORD sanctify Israel, when My sanctuary is among them forever.’”

ruach.gloss: Breath, wind, and Spirit, one Hebrew word doing all three jobs at once. The vision's power depends on this single word's range: the same ruach that gives physical breath is the ruach God puts within His people as His own Spirit.

atsamot_yeveshot.gloss: Dry bones, the picture of total, hopeless death, chosen deliberately as the least likely candidate for life by any natural means, so that only God's own word and Spirit can explain what happens next.

goy_echad.gloss: One nation, ending the division into two kingdoms (Israel and Judah) that had marked Israel's history since Solomon's death, reunited here under one King forever.

basic.context: Ezekiel is set down in a valley littered with very dry bones, the picture of Israel's own despair: 'our bones are dried up, our hope has perished, we are cut off.' At God's command Ezekiel prophesies first to the bones, then to the breath itself, and a vast army stands to life. God explains: this is the whole house of Israel, and He will open their graves, put His own Spirit in them, and settle them in their land. Then two sticks, Judah and Joseph, are joined into one in Ezekiel's hand, picturing a reunited nation under one King, David's Son, forever.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a vision, not a literal event witnessed by others, given to answer Israel's specific despair over the exile with the promise of national restoration; the chapter itself interprets its own symbolism plainly, 'these bones are the whole house of Israel' (v11) and 'these sticks... will become one in My hand' (v19). Its language of graves opened and breath given also anticipates, without exhausting, the New Testament's teaching on bodily resurrection and the gift of the Spirit.
basic.originalLanguages: The vision turns entirely on one word played three ways: ruach, meaning breath, wind, and Spirit all at once. The same ruach that must enter the atsamot yeveshot, dry bones, to give them life is the ruach God promises to put within His people so they will live, verse 14, the very word Genesis uses for the breath of life breathed into Adam, Genesis 2:7. The two sticks joined become goy echad, one nation, ending forever the division into two kingdoms.
basic.typology: Physical resurrection from dry bones is a direct, deliberate preview of bodily resurrection, most fully realized in Christ's own rising, the firstfruits of everyone who belongs to Him, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23, and in the resurrection of all the dead at His return, John 5:28-29. The reuniting of Judah and Joseph into one nation under one King anticipates Christ breaking down the dividing wall of hostility to create one new humanity in Himself, Ephesians 2:14-16.
basic.christ: Jesus is both the source of the ruach that raises dead bones to life, breathing His Spirit on His disciples after the resurrection, John 20:22, and the pattern and guarantee of resurrection itself: 'I am the resurrection and the life,' John 11:25. He is also 'My servant David', the one King appointed forever over the reunited nation, Ezekiel 37:24-25, the Son of David whose kingdom has no end, Luke 1:32-33.
basic.god: God is the God who speaks life into what is beyond hope, dry, disjointed bones with no natural capacity to live, and He does it by His own word and His own Spirit's breath, not by any latent potential in the bones themselves. He is equally the God who reunites what sin and history had divided, Israel and Judah, into one people under one King, sealed by an everlasting covenant and His own presence dwelling among them forever.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2:7 records God breathing the breath of life into Adam, the same word and act echoed here. Ephesians 2:14-16 shows Christ making two into one new humanity. John 11:25 has Jesus declaring Himself the resurrection and the life. 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 grounds all future bodily resurrection in Christ's own.
basic.oneStory: This is Scripture's most vivid picture of hope beyond hope: a valley of dry bones, hopeless by every natural measure, stands up as a living army at God's word and Spirit's breath, and a divided, exiled people is reunited under one King forever. It anticipates and guarantees the resurrection life and undivided people of God secured in Jesus Christ, who is Himself the resurrection, the one Shepherd-King, and the dwelling place of God with man.

context.literary: A vision (verses 1-14) followed by an acted-out sign (verses 15-23) and a closing oracle of covenant promise (verses 24-28), the three together forming the emotional and theological high point of Ezekiel's turn from judgment to restoration.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles who had concluded their national existence was effectively over, 'we are cut off', following the fall of Jerusalem; the vision and the joined sticks both answer the specific despair of a people who assumed reunification and restoration were impossible.
context.canonical: One of Scripture's most vivid resurrection images, later echoed in Jewish and Christian hope for bodily resurrection, and its 'one nation, one king' promise anticipates the New Testament's vision of Jew and Gentile united as one people in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the exiles' total despair with the promise of national restoration pictured as resurrection from the dead, and to promise the reunification of a divided nation under one everlasting King and covenant of peace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is a specific vision and sign addressed to a specific historical despair (the exile, and the division between Israel and Judah); it is not prescribing a ritual. Its enduring, prescriptive truth is that no situation is too dead or too divided for God's word and Spirit to restore.

debates.question: Does the vision of dry bones primarily teach national restoration for Israel from exile, or does it also directly teach individual bodily resurrection?

views.label: Primarily national restoration, with resurrection as the vehicle of the image
views.case: The chapter's own interpretation (v11) identifies the bones as 'the whole house of Israel' and its explicit concern as the exile's despair and the promised return to the land, so many read the vision as using resurrection imagery to picture national restoration, without intending to teach the doctrine of individual bodily resurrection directly.

views.label: An image that also assumes and points to real bodily resurrection
views.case: Others note the vision's power depends on its hearers already believing bodily resurrection is possible and desirable (an idea present elsewhere in the Old Testament, Daniel 12:2, Isaiah 26:19), and that the New Testament treats such imagery as a genuine preview, not merely a borrowed metaphor, of the resurrection Christ secures.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the passage's primary application here is Israel's national restoration; both also agree Scripture's fuller revelation shows that hope resting on this very kind of resurrection power, applied nationally here, is fulfilled bodily and individually in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The vision turns entirely on one word played three ways: ruach, meaning breath, wind, and Spirit all at once. The same ruach that must enter the atsamot yeveshot, dry bones, to give them life is the ruach God promises to put within His people so they will live, verse 14, the very word Genesis uses for the breath of life breathed into Adam, Genesis 2:7. The two sticks joined become goy echad, one nation, ending forever the division into two kingdoms.

typology.type: Dry bones brought to life by the Spirit's breath, Ezekiel 37:1-10
typology.fulfillment: Previews bodily resurrection, guaranteed by Christ's own rising as the firstfruits of all who belong to Him, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23, and consummated at the resurrection of all the dead, John 5:28-29.

typology.type: Two sticks, Judah and Joseph, joined into one nation under one king, Ezekiel 37:15-24
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ making Jew and Gentile one new humanity, breaking down the dividing wall of hostility, Ephesians 2:14-16.

study.christ: Jesus is both the source of the ruach that raises dead bones to life, breathing His Spirit on His disciples after the resurrection, John 20:22, and the pattern and guarantee of resurrection itself: 'I am the resurrection and the life,' John 11:25. He is also 'My servant David', the one King appointed forever over the reunited nation, Ezekiel 37:24-25, the Son of David whose kingdom has no end, Luke 1:32-33, and in whom God's own dwelling is set among His people forever through the Spirit, Ephesians 2:22, until it is consummated when 'the dwelling place of God is with man,' Revelation 21:3.
study.god: God is the God who speaks life into what is beyond hope, dry, disjointed bones with no natural capacity to live, and He does it by His own word and His own Spirit's breath, not by any latent potential in the bones themselves. He is equally the God who reunites what sin and history had divided, Israel and Judah, into one people under one King, sealed by an everlasting covenant and His own presence dwelling among them forever.

crossReferences.note: God breathes the breath (ruach) of life into Adam, the same word and the same divine act echoed when breath enters the dry bones and they live.
Christ makes the two, Jew and Gentile, into one new humanity, reconciling both to God in one body through the cross, fulfilling this chapter's picture of two sticks joined into one.
Jesus declares Himself the resurrection and the life, the person and power behind every dry-bones resurrection this vision anticipates.
Christ's own resurrection is the firstfruits guaranteeing the future bodily resurrection of everyone who belongs to Him, the fullest fulfillment of this vision's promise of life from the grave.

study.oneStory: This is Scripture's most vivid picture of hope beyond hope: a valley of dry bones, hopeless by every natural measure, stands up as a living army at God's word and Spirit's breath, and a divided, exiled people is reunited under one King forever. It anticipates and guarantees the resurrection life and undivided people of God secured in Jesus Christ, who is Himself the resurrection, the one Shepherd-King, and the dwelling place of God with man.

sermon.title: The God Who Makes Alive
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly from Ezekiel 37:1-14, the valley of dry bones, tracing exactly the movement from hopeless death to Spirit-given life this study centers on, and its anticipation of resurrection life secured fully in Christ.

quiz.q: What despairing words does Ezekiel record the exiles saying about themselves, verse 11?

options.text: 'Our bones are dried up, and our hope has perished; we are cut off.'
options.note: Verse 11 records exactly these words as the despair the vision directly answers.

options.text: 'The city has been taken and we are shamed forever.'
options.note: That report belongs to Ezekiel 33:21; this chapter's despair is expressed differently, as hope perished and being cut off.

options.text: 'Our God has abandoned this land completely.'
options.note: The recorded despair concerns the people's own hope and standing, not a claim that God abandoned the land.

options.text: No specific words are recorded; only the vision itself is described.
options.note: The chapter records specific, quoted words of despair directly in verse 11.

quiz.q: In what order does life return to the bones, according to verses 7-10?

options.text: Bones come together, then tendons, flesh, and skin appear, and only afterward does breath enter them to bring them to life.
options.note: Verses 7-10 describe exactly this sequence, physical form first, breath entering last.

options.text: Breath enters first, and the physical body forms around it afterward.
options.note: The sequence is reversed from this; physical form comes first, and breath only afterward brings it to life.

options.text: Everything happens simultaneously in a single instant.
options.note: The text describes a distinct two-stage process, physical assembly and then a separate act of prophesying to the breath.

options.text: The bones are described as never actually receiving physical form, only symbolic hope.
options.note: The bones visibly receive tendons, flesh, and skin, verse 8, before the account of breath entering them.

quiz.q: What does God explicitly promise to do in verse 14, alongside opening the graves?

options.text: Put His Spirit in them so they will live, and settle them in their own land.
options.note: Verse 14 states both promises directly and together.

options.text: Give them a new set of written laws to obey.
options.note: The promise concerns God's own Spirit and resettlement in the land, not a new law code.

options.text: Send a new prophet to replace Ezekiel among them.
options.note: No replacement prophet is mentioned; the promise concerns the Spirit and the land.

options.text: Nothing further is promised beyond opening the graves.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly adds two further promises, the Spirit given and settlement in the land.

quiz.q: What do the two sticks, for Judah and for Joseph/Ephraim, symbolize once joined together, verses 15-22?

options.text: That God will make His people one nation in the land, no longer divided into two kingdoms.
options.note: Verse 22 states this explicitly as the meaning of the joined sticks.

options.text: That the northern and southern kingdoms will remain permanently separate but allied.
options.note: The text explicitly promises they will 'no longer be two nations,' the opposite of remaining separate.

options.text: That the ten lost tribes would never be found or restored.
options.note: The sign promises the opposite: full reunification of all the tribes into one nation.

options.text: The sticks are purely decorative and carry no symbolic meaning.
options.note: The chapter explicitly interprets the sticks' meaning in verses 19 and 22; they are not merely decorative.

quiz.q: Who is appointed as king over the reunited nation forever, and what covenant does God make with them, verses 24-26?

options.text: God's servant David is king and shepherd forever, under an everlasting covenant of peace with God's sanctuary set among them forever.
options.note: Verses 24-26 state each of these elements directly and together.

options.text: A council of elders governs the nation collaboratively without any king.
options.note: A specific individual king, 'My servant David,' is named directly, not a council.

options.text: Ezekiel himself is appointed as the permanent ruling prophet-king.
options.note: The appointed king is named as 'My servant David,' not Ezekiel.

options.text: No king or covenant is mentioned in this section.
options.note: Both a specific king and a specific everlasting covenant are named explicitly in verses 24-26.

questions: The exiles said, 'our hope has perished; we are cut off,' yet God had resurrection life waiting for them all along. What situation in your life feels like dry bones, beyond any natural hope of recovery?

God's Spirit had to enter the reassembled bodies before they could truly live; flesh and form alone were not enough. Where in your own life have you tried to arrange all the right pieces without depending on God's own Spirit to bring actual life?

Two divided, once-hostile groups, Judah and Joseph, are joined into one people in Ezekiel's own hand at God's command. What division, in your family, church, or community, do you need to trust God to heal rather than resigning yourself to permanent separation?

God promises His own sanctuary, His dwelling, will be among His people forever. How does knowing God's own presence, not just His promises, is what secures your future change how you face an uncertain day?
This vision answers total despair with total, resurrection-level hope. Where do you need to preach this same promise to your own heart this week rather than simply agreeing with it as a nice idea?

translationNotes.note: 'By His Spirit' translates the same Hebrew word, ruach, rendered 'breath' and 'wind' later in the chapter; the single word's range (breath, wind, Spirit) is essential to the vision's meaning and cannot be fully captured by any single English word throughout.

'We are cut off' uses a Hebrew verb often applied to being cut off from the covenant community entirely; the exiles' despair was not merely emotional but a fear of total covenant exclusion, which God's promise directly answers.

The 'stick' in this acted-out sign was likely a flat wooden tablet suitable for writing a name on and holding alongside another, a simple visual aid Ezekiel could join together in one hand before the watching exiles.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. Sermon link verified via live web search and fetch this session, Gospel in Life (Tim Keller), 'The God Who Makes Alive', preached directly from Ezekiel 37:1-14, matching this study's central passage.
